titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: The Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island

rs.persName: The Lone Woman of San Nicholas Island

div.p: Seventy-five miles off the Southern California coast, the small island of San Nicholas served as a bombing range for the U.S. Navy in World War II. Later, the Navy targeted the island while testing its secret guided missile. Today the rocky isle is home to sea lions, seals, walrus and several species of birds.

It was far different in 1602, when Spanish explorer Sebastian Vizcaino discovered San Nicholas, then populated by a large tribe of Native Americans who lived there in contentment and isolation from the mainland. But by the early 19th century, the island was rediscovered by hunters who decimated the seal and otter population and much of the Indian population as well. In 1835, friars at the San Gabriel Mission decided the San Nicholas Indians would be better off living on the mainland, and dispatched Captain Charles Hubbard to round them up and return them to Los Angeles.

As the islanders were boarding the captain's boat, one young woman cried out that her baby had been left behind, and jumped overboard. The captain decided chasing after her was too much trouble and ordered the vessel to sail.

Fifteen years later, two hunters, Thomas Detman and George Nidever, trekked to San Nicholas to shoot seal and otter. The hunters' Indian guide mentioned he'd seen a naked woman running in the hills, a tale they chalked up to a case of "squaw fever." But in 1853, when Nidever and Detman again returned to San Nicholas, they discovered small, human footprints in the sand, and went in search of the mystery woman. For three weeks she eluded them until, by accident, the hunters finally found her. Her camp was so well hidden, Nidever and Detman would have passed it had her dog not growled. When she finally emerged from her hideout, she made quite an impression: She was splendidly clothed in a dress of shimmering green cormorant feathers.

The next day Nidever and Detman led her onto their boat and sailed for Santa Barbara. While at the mission it was discovered that Native Americans, including second-generation members of her tribe, could not converse with her, so unusual was her dialect. As a result, the fate of the baby she jumped ship to save remained a mystery. Within a month the woman, in perfect health when found, was too weak to walk. She had lived for 18 years on raw abalone and fish and could not tolerate the rich mission food. Within three months she was dead.

Before she died, the woman was baptized and named Juana Maria. Her green feather dress, replaced with "sensible attire," was sent to the Pope where it remains in the Vatican Museum. In 1928, the Daughters of the American Revolution installed a plaque on her grave at the Santa Barbara Mission, where it can be seen today.
Woody McBride is a Fedco staff writer.
